title: Flat band in multiband-metal MnSb$_2$
authors: Carl Jonas Linnemann, Kim-Khuong Huynh, Davide Ceresoli, Martin Bremholm
abstract: Marcasite compounds formed between $3d$ transition metals and antimony (TMSb$_2$) have been heavily studied due to their intriguing physical properties. For instance they can possess flat bands in their electronic structure, however due to their semiconducting nature, these intriguing electronic states often reside far from the Fermi level, and observations of their properties remained elusive. In addition, the studies of the marcasite series is incomplete across the $3d$ TMs as the electronic and physical properties of MnSb$_2$ are little studied, as its synthesis requires the application of pressure. We successfully used a high-pressure approach to obtain MnSb$_2$, the last TMSb$_2$ that was still missing, and confirm its marcasite structure. The results of our measurements of electronic transport properties are consistent with the manifestation of a flat band that resides at the vicinity of the Fermi level and being in good agreement with the DFT band structure.
summary: This paper reports the first successful synthesis of the marcasite compound MnSb2 using high-pressure techniques. The authors demonstrate that, unlike other members of its family which are semiconductors, MnSb2 is a multi-band metal. Crucially, they identify a flat band located at the Fermi level, which leads to heavy-electron behavior. The study also details several temperature-dependent phase transitions and transport anomalies.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the electronic and transport properties of MnSb2, a previously under-investigated member of the TMSb2 marcasite family, and to determine if it possesses a flat band at the Fermi level.
main_result: The researchers successfully synthesized MnSb2 using high pressure and confirmed the presence of a flat band at the Fermi level, making it the first marcasite in this series to exhibit such a feature at the Fermi energy.
method: The study combines high-pressure/high-temperature synthesis with structural characterization (SC-XRD, P-XRD), electronic transport measurements (resistivity, Hall effect, Seebeck, Nernst), heat capacity, and DFT calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is MnSb2, a multi-band metal with an orthorhombic marcasite structure. It is characterized by Mn 3d orbitals and is predicted to have an antiferromagnetic ground state.
key_observables: Electrical resistivity, Hall resistivity, Seebeck coefficient, Nernst signal, heat capacity, Sommerfeld constant (gamma), and lattice parameters.
important_parameters: Temperature-dependent phase transitions at 230 K, 110 K, and 30 K; Sommerfeld constant gamma = 17.4 mJ/(mol K^2); carrier masses (electron-like mass approx 11me).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the two-band model for extracting carrier dynamics and uses DFT to model the electronic band structure and magnetic stability.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the calculated electronic band structure, Brillouin zone, and Fermi surface; Figure 3 displays measured physical properties including heat capacity and resistivity.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the synthesis and structural characterization of MnSb2, followed by an investigation of its electronic transport and thermodynamic properties, and concludes with a comparison between experimental results and DFT calculations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of MnSb2, showing the MnSb6 octahedron with axial and equatorial Mn-Sb bonds. (b) Ex- tracted unit cell parameters from SCXRD showing a kink at 230 K. (c) Mn-Sb bond lengths (d) Bond angle α.

FIG. 2. (a) Calculated electronic band structure, with colors indicating the contributions from Mn 3d orbitals, and the total and atomic projected density of states. (b) Brillouin zone and the path used in the band structure (c-d) Fermi surface of band 2, with colors marking the contributions from the distinct bands.

FIG. 3. Measured physical properties of MnSb2: (a) Heat capacity, with the insert showing the fit to the low temperature heat capacity. (b) Resistivity ρxx. (c) Seebeck coefficient with a linear fit to the low temperature Seebeck coefficient. (d) Magnetoresistivity at selected temperatures. (e) All measured ρyx. The measured curves closest to the observed transitions are marked in black. f) Nernst signal. The vertical lines in (a-c) mark the observed transitions, while in (e) the lines serve as a guide to the eye.

FIG. 4. Derived properties from ρyx and the Nernst coeffi- cient. (a) Nernst coefficient ν showing a sign switching below 30 K together with the Hall coefficient extracted from a lin- ear fit between ±1 T to the low field ρyx. (b) Carrier numbers extracted from ρyx. (c) Mobilities extracted from ρyx as well as the average mobility extracted from the low field ρxx.
